title: Corridor Lights After Lights-Out
statement_md:
The hostel warden is in a mood tonight. The corridor has `N` lights, numbered `1` to `N`, and all of them start **OFF**.

The warden makes `N` passes down the corridor. On pass `i` (for `i = 1` to `N`), he flips the switch of every `i`-th light — lights `i`, `2i`, `3i`, and so on. Flipping a switch turns an OFF light ON and an ON light OFF.

After all `N` passes are done, how many lights are ON?
input_format_md:
The first line contains a single integer `T` — the number of test cases.

Each of the next `T` lines contains a single integer `N` — the number of lights in the corridor.
output_format_md: For each test case, print a single line with one integer — the number of lights that are ON after all `N` passes.
constraints_md:
- `1 ≤ T ≤ 10`
- `1 ≤ N ≤ 10^18`
- careful: `N` does not fit in a 32-bit integer — use 64-bit (`long` / `long long`) or `BigInt`

starter_code.python:
import sys

def solve_case(n):
    # your code here — return how many lights are ON
    # note: n can be as large as 10**18
    return 0

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    t = int(data[0])
    for i in range(1, t + 1):
        n = int(data[i])
        print(solve_case(n))

main()

reference_solution.python:
import sys
import math

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.buffer.read().split()
    t = int(data[0])
    out = []
    for i in range(1, t + 1):
        n = int(data[i])
        out.append(str(math.isqrt(n)))
    print("\n".join(out))

main()

hints: Light number `L` gets flipped once for every pass number that divides `L` exactly. So a light ends up ON only if `L` has an odd number of divisors. List the divisors of 12, then of 16 — what is different about 16?

Divisors come in pairs (`d` with `L/d`), so the count is even — unless one divisor pairs with itself. Count how many numbers from 1 to `N` have that property. And since `N` goes up to 10^18, stay in integers: floating-point square roots WILL be off by one on the biggest cases.
